Reference temperature from substation group analysis (Wrocław)¶

Objective: Compare Foreca temperatures with controller temperatures for the selected winter period (Feb 4, 2026) and evaluate whether aggregated controller temperatures can serve as a stable reference signal. Data for analysis was taken on a cloudy day.¶

Total number of substations registered in CRM for Wroclaw.¶

6687

Wrocław Substations Used in Analysis¶

Only substations with coordinates were used for further spatial analysis.¶

Number of unique substations in the file: 6055

Wrocław substations in CRM: 6687
Wrocław substations with coordinates: 6055
Missing coordinates: 632

Data Cleaning and Exclusions¶

OTZ substations (Wrocław only): 356
KIONA substations (Wrocław only): 45
Total excluded (Wrocław only): 399
Filtered Wrocław pool: 6055 -> 5656 (removed 399)

Data Quality Assessment Before Analysis¶

array([-1], dtype=int64)
temperature
0.0    20
1.0     3
Name: count, dtype: int64

Physical Level — Summary¶

From 5656 Wrocław substations, 5 (0.09%) recorded physically impossible temperatures (< -40°C or > 60°C).¶

These substations are marked for exclusion at the physical filtering stage.¶

APPLYING PHYSICAL FILTER TO WROCŁAW POOL
Wrocław pool before physical filter: 5656
Removed at physical level: 5
Wrocław pool after physical filter: 5651
Share removed at physical level: 0.09 %

Physical Filtering — Example¶

Example of a substation excluded due to physically impossible controller temperature values.

Tail Anomaly Exclusion - Summary¶

125 substations (2.21%) were excluded at the logical level due to abnormal temperature bias behaviour (persistent statistical tail behaviour detected via IQR-based method).¶

The remaining clean pool contains 5526 substations.¶

LOGICAL LEVEL REPORT
Wrocław pool before logical filter: 5651
Substations removed at logical level: 125
Share removed at logical level: 2.21 %
Wrocław pool after logical filter: 5526

Controller vs Foreca Bias Analysis (Final Clean Pool)¶

The following analysis evaluates temperature bias behaviour for substations that passed all filtering stages (OTZ/KIONA removal, physical validation, and logical anomaly exclusion).

Controller vs Foreca deviation is computed using ±30 min anchor window for all substations that passed physical and logical filtering.

foreca_9 size: 24
ctrl_df size: 357391
Unique foreca timestamps: 24

Technical Summary (data coverage)¶

Active substations with matched Controller–Foreca data: 3462
Controller–Foreca comparison records used for analysis: 82907
Controller minus Foreca temperature range: -6.2 to 9.3

Record-level distribution of all Controller - Foreca differences¶

Node-level bias ranking (mean delta per substation)¶

Distribution of mean bias across substations¶

The boxplot illustrates the distribution of mean temperature bias across substations. The median bias is slightly positive, indicating that controller readings tend to be marginally higher than Foreca estimates. Most substations exhibit moderate bias, with a small number of higher positive and negative outliers.

Logical-level filtering (IQR-based)¶

Substations with extreme mean bias values were removed using an IQR-based method.

Lower bound: -4.0°C
Upper bound: 4.4°C

Resulting clean pool size: 5526 substations.

Anomalous substations loaded (unique): 1074
WRO pool (after OTZ/KIONA): 5651
Anomalous substations inside WRO pool: 125
Rows: before -> after: 5651 -> 5526 (removed 125 )
Final clean pool size: 5526

The map highlights 100 substations selected using a 10×10 spatial grid to ensure representative coverage across Wrocław.

Representative Substations by Deviation Level¶

Three representative substations were selected from the full clean pool (after all filtering) based on aggregated mean bias across the analysed period (mean Δ):

  • Substation with bias closest to zero
  • Substation with typical (median) bias
  • Substation with largest absolute bias (strongest deviation)
['911166', '901151', '911368']

Three representative substations were selected based on aggregated mean bias across the analysed period: closest to zero, median bias, and largest absolute deviation.

Mean Temperature Deviation Distribution¶

To evaluate bias at the city level, we analyse:

  • distribution of mean bias across substations
  • proportion of substations within deviation ranges
  • average daily temperature curves (all substations vs grid sample)

Note: Mean bias is calculated only for substations with valid controller–Foreca matches on the selected day (3462 out of 5526 clean substations).

2. Extreme Negative Bias - Maximum Controller Undercooling¶

Anchor-Window Extreme Deviation Analysis¶

The chart shows the share of substations that had at least one anchor-window extreme deviation beyond the statistical thresholds (> 4.4°C or < -4.0°C) during the analyzed day.

Total substations with anchor data: 3462

Overheated substations (max controller-foreca > 4.4°C): 68 (2.0%)
Undercooled substations (min controller-foreca < -4.0°C): 33 (1.0%)
Substations with both extreme spikes: 0

Interpretation¶

2% of substations exhibited at least one extreme positive anchor-window deviation (> +4.4°C), while only 1% showed extreme negative deviation (< −4.0°C).

Approximately 97.1% of substations remained within the defined deviation bounds throughout the analysed day.

This distribution indicates that extreme anchor-window spikes are rare and isolated events rather than widespread systematic issues. The temperature bias behaviour appears stable under cloudy winter conditions.
